sources.notes: Read statement and constraints: same hidden RBS oracle as C1, but query limit is 200.
Used official editorial D1C1/C2/C3. Medium version improves batching using binary-code-like query templates.

reference_candidates.notes: Candidate Luogu solution page; not consulted in this review.

annotation.summary: Interactive reconstruction of a hidden bracket sequence under a tighter 200-query limit.
annotation.constraints: n <= 1000, at most 200 queries, k <= 1000, repeated indices allowed.
annotation.core_idea: As in C1, first find one '(' anchor and one ')' anchor. The tighter limit requires batching more than two positions in a single query: arrange the anchors and several unknown positions so that each candidate contributes a distinct binary-weighted amount to f(t) when it is '('. The returned integer is decoded as a small bitmask, recovering a block of positions per query.
annotation.complexity: O(log n + n / b) queries for a small constant block size b, within 200; O(n) local decoding.
tricks: Use known anchors to isolate each unknown position's contribution.
Encode several positions in one numeric response by assigning distinguishable binary weights.

solution_variants.name: binary-coded-block-queries
solution_variants.summary: Recover a constant-size block per query by making each unknown position contribute a different power-of-two value to f(t).
solution_variants.complexity: O(log n + n / b) queries, within 200

tags.evidence: The entire solution is an adaptive query strategy under a fixed query budget.
The oracle query is deliberately shaped so the count f(t) reveals multiple hidden brackets at once.
tags.description: Designing queries to an oracle/interactor so that the numeric or Boolean response directly encodes the desired hidden information.
tags.created_reason: The broad interactive tag does not distinguish problems whose main difficulty is constructing a decoding oracle rather than binary search or randomized probing.

tags.evidence: Several bracket values are packed into one integer answer by giving their contributions distinct binary weights.
tags.description: Encoding multiple hidden decisions into one numeric/query response using unique weights, positional codes, or other decodable representations.
tags.created_reason: Interactive and reconstruction tasks often hinge on how information is packed into few oracle answers; this is more specific than generic bitmask or constructive tags.

tags.evidence: The medium version decodes the answer as a small bitmask over a block of positions.
Binary search is used to find a useful pair of opposite bracket anchors before block decoding.
The method constructs special query strings to realize the required code.
